blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (١)
The Catastrophe! (1)
مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (٢)
What is the Catastrophe? (2)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْقَارِعَةُ (٣)
What will show you what is the Catastrophe? (3)
يَوْمَ يَكُونُ ٱلنَّاسُ كَٱلْفَرَاشِ ٱلْمَبْثُوثِ (٤)
[It is] the day when mankind will be like scattered moths (4)
وَتَكُونُ ٱلْجِبَالُ كَٱلْعِهْنِ ٱلْمَنفُوشِ (٥)
and the mountains like carded wool. (5)
فَأَمَّا مَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ (٦)
As for him, whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, (6)
فَهُوَ فِى عِيشَةٍ رَّاضِيَةٍ (٧)
he will have a pleasing life. (7)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَٰزِينُهُۥ (٨)
But as for him, whose deeds weigh light in the scales, (8)
فَأُمُّهُۥ هَاوِيَةٌ (٩)
his home will be the Abyss. (9)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا هِيَهْ (١٠)
And what will show you what it is? (10)
نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌۢ (١١)
It is a scorching fire! (11)

Content and Virtue of Surah Al-Qari'ah

This Surah generally speaks of the Resurrection and its precursors with striking expressions, a shaking tone, and explicit, clear warnings. Ultimately, it divides humanity into two groups: those whose deeds are heavy in the scale of Divine Justice, whose reward is a life entirely satisfying in the proximity of God's mercy; and those whose deeds are light and weightless, whose destiny is the hot and scorching fire of Hell.

The name of this Surah, "Al-Qari'ah," is taken from its first verse.

Regarding its virtue, it is sufficient to mention a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام:

من قرأ القارعة آمنه اللَّه من فتنة الدجال ان يؤمن به، و من قيح جهنم يوم القيامة ان شاء اللَّه:

"Whoever recites Surah Al-Qari'ah, Allah the Almighty will secure him from the fitna (trial) of the Dajjal and from believing in him, and He will keep him away from the purulence of Hell on the Day of Resurrection, God willing." [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 530.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ الْقَارِعَةُ (1) مَا الْقَارِعَةُ (2) وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا الْقَارِعَةُ (3) يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَرَاشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ (4) وَتَكُونُ الْجِبَالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ (5) فَأَمَّا مَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ (6) فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ رَاضِيَةٍ (7) وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ (8) فَأُمُّهُ هَاوِيَةٌ (9) وَمَا أَدْرَاكَ مَا هِيَهْ (10) نَارٌ حَامِيَةٌ (11)

Commentary

The Striking Event!

In these verses describing the Resurrection, it first says: "The Striking Event" (الْقَارِعَةُ).

"What is the Striking Event?" (مَا الْقَارِعَةُ).

"And what will make you realize what the Striking Event is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْقارِعَةُ).

"Qari'ah" is derived from the root qar‘ (rhyming with far‘), meaning to strike one thing against another in a way that produces a loud sound. A whip or a hammer is called miqra'ah for this reason. Furthermore, any significant and harsh calamity is called qari'ah (the feminine 'ta' here may indicate emphasis).

From the expressions used in the second and third verses—where it even asks the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, "What will make you realize what that harsh and striking event is?"—it becomes clear that this event is so immense that its dimensions cannot be grasped by anyone's thought.

In any case, many commentators have stated that Al-Qari'ah is one of the names of the Resurrection. However, they have not clearly specified whether this term refers to the precursors of the Resurrection—when the world is crushed, the sun and moon are darkened, and the seas are set ablaze (if so, the reason for choosing the name Al-Qari'ah for this event is clear)—or if it refers to the second stage, namely the stage of the dead being resurrected and the new design of the universe. In the latter case, the term "Striking" would be because the horror, fear, and dread of that day strike the hearts.

Some of the verses that follow are consistent with the event of the world's destruction, while others are consistent with the resurrection of the dead. Overall, the first possibility seems more appropriate, although in these verses, both events are mentioned one after the other (as in many other Quranic verses that report on the Resurrection).

Then, in describing that wondrous day, it says: "The day when mankind will be like scattered moths," wandering and bewildered in every direction (يَوْمَ يَكُونُ النَّاسُ كَالْفَراشِ الْمَبْثُوثِ).

Farash is the plural of farashah. Many consider it to mean "moth," while some have interpreted it as "locust." Apparently, this latter meaning is taken from verse 7 of Surah Al-Qamar, which likens people on that day to scattered locusts (كَأَنَّهُمْ جَرادٌ مُنْتَشِرٌ); otherwise, its literal meaning is "moth."

In any case, the comparison to "moths" is because moths usually throw themselves madly into the fire and burn; similarly, the evildoers throw themselves into the fire of Hell.

It is also possible that the comparison to moths refers to the specific bewilderment and confusion that will overcome all humans on that day.

If farash means locusts, the comparison is because it is said that many birds fly in a specific path during collective movement, except for locusts, which have no specific path in their group movement and each moves in a different direction.

Again, the question arises: is this bewilderment, confusion, scattering, horror, and anxiety due to the terrifying events of the end of the world, or the beginning of the Resurrection and the Gathering? The answer to this question becomes clear from what we mentioned above.

Then, moving to another description of that day, it adds: "And the mountains will be like carded wool" (وَ تَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ).

‘Ihn (rhyming with zehn) means dyed wool. Manfush is from the root nafsh (rhyming with naqsh), meaning to spread wool, which is usually done with special tools for carding and fluffing wool.

We have previously mentioned that according to various Quranic verses, the mountains will first be set in motion at the threshold of the Resurrection, then crushed and disintegrated, and finally become like dust in the sky. In the verse under discussion, they are likened to carded dyed wool—wool that moves with a gale, with only its color remaining visible. This is the final stage of the mountains' disintegration.

This expression might also refer to the different colors of the mountains, as each mountain on earth has its own specific color. In any case, this sentence is evidence that the above verses speak of the initial stages of the Resurrection, namely the stage of destruction and the end of the world.

Then, moving to the stage of the Gathering, the resurrection of the dead, and their division into two groups, it says: "Then as for him whose scales [of deeds] are heavy..." (فَأَمَّا مَنْ ثَقُلَتْ مَوازِينُهُ).

"He will be in a life well-pleasing" (فَهُوَ فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ).

"But as for him whose scales are light..." (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ خَفَّتْ مَوازِينُهُ).

"His abode and refuge will be the Pit (Hawiyah)" (فَأُمُّهُ هاوِيَةٌ).

"And what will make you realize what 'Hawiyah' and Hell is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما هِيَهْ) [1]

"It is a scorching Fire!" (نارٌ حامِيَةٌ).

Mawazin is the plural of mizan, meaning a means of measurement. This word was first used for physical weights and then applied to spiritual measures and standards.

Some believe that human deeds on that day will take on physical forms and become weighable, and they will truly be weighed with scales for measuring deeds.

The possibility has also been raised that the records of deeds themselves are weighed; if righteous deeds are written in them, they are heavy; otherwise, they are light or weightless.

However, there is apparently no need for these justifications. Mizan does not necessarily mean a physical scale with specific pans; rather, it applies to any means of measurement. As we read in a Hadith:

ان امير المؤمنين و الأئمة من ذريته (ع) هم الموازين:

"Indeed, the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams from his progeny عليهم السلام are the scales." [2] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when asked about the meaning of mizan, he replied: الميزان العدل! "The scale is Justice!" [1]

[1]: "Ma hiyah" was originally "ma hiya," then the "ha of silence" (ha al-sakt) was appended to it. [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 7, p. 251.

Thus, the existence of the Friends of Allah (Awliya) or the laws of Divine Justice are the standards against which humans and their deeds are presented; their weight is determined by the extent to which they resemble and correspond to them! It is clear that "heaviness" and "lightness" of the scale do not refer to the weight of the scales themselves, but to the weight of the things being measured by them.

Furthermore, the use of mawazin in the plural form is because the guardians of truth and the Divine laws are each a scale of measurement. Moreover, the diversity of human attributes and deeds requires that each be measured with a specific scale, and thus the models and standards of measurement are varied.

Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "In the Holy Quran, mizan appears sometimes in the singular and sometimes in the plural. In the first case, it refers to the One who calculates, namely the One God; in the second case, it refers to those being calculated."

Some commentators have also said that mawazin is the plural of mawzun, meaning the deed that is weighed. Therefore, the heaviness or lightness of the scales means the heaviness or lightness of the deeds themselves, not the scales [2]. Of course, the result of both is the same, but through two different paths.

We have provided more detailed explanations on this subject under verses 8 and 9 of Surah Al-A'raf (Vol. 6, p. 90), as well as under verse 105 of Al-Kahf (Vol. 12, p. 567) and verse 102 of Surah Al-Mu'minun (Vol. 14, p. 328).

The expression ‘ishatin radiyah (a well-pleasing life) is a very beautiful and eloquent description of the life full of blessings and complete peace for the people of Paradise in the Resurrection. This life is so satisfying that it is as if it is "pleased" itself; that is, instead of saying mardiyyah (pleased with), the "active participle" (ism al-fa'il) is used instead of the "passive participle" (ism al-maf'ul) for maximum emphasis [1]. This great distinction belongs specifically to the life of the Hereafter, for no matter how comfortable, blessed, peaceful, and satisfying the life of this world may be, it is never free from factors of dissatisfaction. Only the life of the Hereafter is entirely satisfaction, pleasure, peace, security, and a source of tranquility.

The use of umm (mother) in the phrase fa-ummuhu hawiyah is because umm means "mother," and we know that a mother is a refuge for children to whom they flee in times of trouble and with whom they stay. Here, it indicates that these sinners with light scales find no place for refuge except Hell. Woe to the one whose refuge is "Hell"!

Some have also said that umm here means "brain," as the Arabs call the brain umm al-ra's (the mother of the head). Therefore, the meaning of the verse would be that they are thrown headlong into Hell. However, this possibility seems unlikely because, in that case, the subsequent verse—

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 2, p. 5. [2]: This meaning was mentioned by Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, Fakhr al-Razi in Al-Tafsir al-Kabir, and Abu al-Futuh al-Razi in his commentary as one of two possibilities for the meaning of mawazin.

"And what will make you realize what it is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما هِيَهْ)—would not have a proper meaning.

Hawiyah is from the root hawa, meaning to fall or drop, and it is one of the names of "Hell," because sinners fall into it, and it also refers to the depth of the fire of Hell.

If we take umm here to mean "brain," then hawiyah would mean "falling." However, the first interpretation seems more correct and appropriate.

Hamiyah is from the root hama (rhyming with nafi), meaning intensity of heat. Hamiyah here refers to the extraordinary burning nature of the fire of Hell.

In any case, the sentence "And what will make you realize what Hawiyah is? It is a scorching Fire" is an emphasis on the fact that the punishment of the Resurrection and the fire of Hell are beyond the imagination of all humans.

Key Point: Causes for the Heaviness of the Scale of Deeds

Undoubtedly, the value of all good deeds and righteous acts is not the same; they differ greatly. For this reason, in various Islamic narrations, more emphasis has been placed on certain good deeds, and they have been considered causes for the heaviness of the scale of deeds in the Resurrection.

Among these, in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read regarding the interpretation of La ilaha illa Allah:

يعنى بوحدانيته لا يقبل اللَّه الاعمال الا بها، و هى كلمة التقوى يثقل اللَّه بها الموازين يوم القيامة

"It refers to His Oneness; Allah does not accept deeds except through it. It is the word of Taqwa (piety) by which Allah makes the scales heavy on the Day of Resurrection." [1]

In another Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding the testimony to the Oneness of Allah and the Prophethood of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is said:

خف ميزان ترفعان منه و ثقل ميزان توضعان فيه:

"The scale from which the two testimonies (Shahadatayn) are removed is light, and the scale in which the two testimonies are placed is heavy." [2]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام or Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

ما فى الميزان شىء اثقل من الصلاة على محمد و آل محمد

"There is nothing in the scale heavier than sending blessings (Salawat) upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad." At the end of the narration, he says: "Individuals will stand at the scale of deeds on the Day of Resurrection and their pan of deeds will be light; then the blessings upon Muhammad and the family of Muhammad will be placed in it, and it will become heavy." [3]

Interestingly, in a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read:

من كان ظاهره ارجح من باطنه خف ميزانه!:

"Whoever's outward appearance is better than his inward reality, his scale of deeds will be light on the Day of Resurrection!" [4]

We conclude this discussion with a statement from Salman al-Farsi, which is in fact the essence of Revelation and Sunnah. In this Hadith, we read that someone mockingly said to Salman: "Who are you? And what are you? You have no value." Salman replied:

اما اولى و اولك فنطفة قذرة و اما آخرى و آخرك فجيفة منتنة فاذا كان يوم القيامة و نصبت الموازين فمن ثقلت موازينه فهو الكريم و من خفت موازينه فهو اللئيم:

"As for my beginning and yours, it was a filthy drop (sperm), and as for my end and yours, it is nothing but a foul-smelling carcass. When the Day of Resurrection arrives and the scales of deeds are set up, whoever's scale of deeds is heavy is the noble and honorable one, and whoever's scale of deeds is light is the base and ignoble one." [1]

O Allah! Make our scale of deeds heavy with the love of Muhammad and the family of Muhammad.

O Lord! Attaining a "well-pleasing life" is not possible except through Your grace; help us in this path.

O God! The fire of Your Hell is intensely scorching and we have no power to endure it; extinguish it for us with the water of Your mercy and generosity.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Qari'ah.

[1] & [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 659, Hadith 12 and 8. [3]: Ibid, Hadith 7. [4]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 660, Hadith 13. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 660, Hadith 14.
